fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, some of the coolest work going in proof theory at the moment works differently here; it is sensitive to the number of occurrences of an open assumption. We won't go into it in these notes, but I can't come this close to the idea without mentioning it; there's too much good stuff over here! If you're interested in learning more, you might start by searching "substructural logic" or "linear logic", or checking out the Stanford Encyclopedia entry on substructural logic. (This article is by Greg Restall, a well-known Australian logician. Greg was one of the people I was coming to study with when I first came to Australia. Alas, he now lives in Scotland.)
For now, though, let's move on to our remaining connectives. Implication has one introduction rule and one elimination rule:
As you can see, the rule \rightarrow I involves discharge, and the rule \rightarrow E does not.

Let's start with the simpler \rightarrow E, then. This rule says that whenever you have a proof of an implication, and you also have a proof of its antecedent, then you can put those proofs together with a step of \rightarrow E, giving you a proof of the consequent. For example, here is a proof that uses \rightarrow E:

This proof has the open assumption (p \rightarrow (q \land t)) \land p, and from that draws the conclusion t. Note that, although the proof includes two occurrences of the open assumption (p \rightarrow (q \land t)) \land p, when I'm listing out the open assumptions of the proof I don't bother to list it twice. For our purposes, something is either assumed in a proof or it's not; we're not keeping track of number of occurrences.

However, some of the coolest work going in proof theory at the moment works differently here; it is sensitive to the number of occurrences of an open assumption. We won't go into it in these notes, but I can't come this close to the idea without mentioning it; there's too much good stuff over here! If you're interested in learning more, you might start by searching "substructural logic" or "linear logic", or checking out the Stanford Encyclopedia entry on substructural logic. (This article is by Greg Restall, a well-known Australian logician. Greg was one of the people I was coming to study with when I first came to Australia. Alas, he now lives in Scotland.)

Unlike \rightarrow E, \rightarrow I is a discharging rule. To see why, it helps to think about implication in a new kind of way. You might recall from earlier that it's difficult to give what seems like a good truth table for implication. But now that we're working with proofs, we don't actually need to say anything about truth values at all in order to give meaning to our connectives. This gives us a different kind of way to explore the meaning of a connective like implication.

An influential theory of implication holds that an implication like p \rightarrow q has the same kind of meaning as a proof of q that assumes p. On this kind of theory, to believe p \rightarrow q is to believe in some path of reasoning that can take you from p to q.

This kind of theory of "if...then" sentences has been around for a while, but nowadays is gaining new life by connecting up to cognitive science pretty directly. Interesting work in this direction is being done by a number of researchers. One particularly excellent researcher in this area is Karolina Krzyżanowska; if you're interested I'd encourage you to check out some of the papers on her website: http://karolinakrzyzanowska.com.

It's this kind of theory of implication that's encoded in our rules. For the rule \rightarrow E, look at it this way: if we have a proof of p \rightarrow q, then that licenses us to reason from p to q. So if we also have a proof of p, then we can combine the proofs we have to get a proof of q. For the rule \rightarrow I, look at it this way: what it should take to prove p \rightarrow q is to produce a proof of q from the assumption p. In this process, you take p for granted in the course of the proof of q; but once you produce that proof, your final conclusion is not q itself, but just that p implies q. As you don't any longer assume p when you draw this final conclusion, you can discharge the assumption. There are other ways we might encode a theory like this into proof rules; but this is at least one standard way to do so.

That, anyway, is the kind of view of implication that lies behind these rules: thinking of implication as recording in a single sentence the same kind of connection between distinct sentences that we build up in the course of a proof. So, for example, we can have a proof that uses \rightarrow I like so:
This proof is the first example we've seen of a proof with no open assumptions. The total proof takes nothing at all for granted, and still produces a proof of (p \land q) \rightarrow p.

Of course, when I say it takes nothing for granted, I'm not taking account of the rules themselves! These might seem to come from nowhere, and there's definitely an important sense in which we've been taking them for granted as well. Changing the rules gives us different proof systems. So when I say we're taking nothing for granted in this proof, I mean within the current proof system. Like any other aspect of logic, though, we're always free to choose or build a different system!

fr:title.text: Correct proof: extra assumptions, multiple discharge

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Correct proof: extra assumptions, multiple discharge

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This proof demonstrates a few things about discharge. First, note the presence of the open assumption r. This doesn't interfere with the discharge steps at all; indeed, it doesn't interact in any way with them. Discharging p and p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) both go ahead just fine, even though there is another open assumption hanging around. Discharges work like this in general: whether you're allowed to discharge a sentence never depends on what other sentences you have as open assumptions.

Second, note that at the step marked ^2 this proof discharges two instances of the assumption p. That's fine: when you're allowed to discharge a sentence, you're allowed to discharge any number of copies of that sentence. (This is another case where doing things differently would lead us into the fascinating world of substructural logics.)
